rdb-save-incremental-fsync yes
activedefrag yes

sections.title: Anti-Patterns & Common Mistakes
sections.description: Frequent pitfalls and anti-patterns that can degrade Redis performance, reliability, and security — and how to avoid them.

items.label: Using KEYS * in Production
items.text:
KEYS * blocks the Redis event loop for the entire duration of the scan. On a dataset with millions of keys, this can block all operations for seconds or even minutes — causing timeouts, cascading failures, and Sentinel failovers.

✅ Fix: Always use SCAN (cursor-based iteration) instead:
SCAN 0 MATCH user:* COUNT 100

In Redis 7.4+, you can also configure the max iterations for SCAN.

items.label: Storing Massive Blobs / Large Keys
items.text:
Storing multi-megabyte values in Redis causes:
• Network congestion — transferring large payloads blocks the connection
• Latency spikes — serialization/deserialization of large objects is slow
• Memory fragmentation — allocating large blocks stresses jemalloc
• Blocked replicas — replication streams large values synchronously

✅ Fix: Break large objects into hashes (individual fields for each attribute). Redis is optimized for many small keys, not few large ones. The recommended max value size is 10-100 KB per key.

items.label: Using Redis as the Primary Database
items.text:
Redis is an in-memory store — if you lose the server (crash, OOM kill, hardware failure), you lose data. Even with AOF+RDB persistence, the recovery window means data loss is possible.

✅ Fix: Use Redis as a cache layer in front of a durable database (PostgreSQL, MySQL, etc.). Write to the database first, then populate Redis. Never treat Redis as your source of truth unless you fully understand the persistence and replication tradeoffs.

items.label: Shared Instances (Noisy Neighbor)
items.text:
Using a single Redis instance for multiple microservices creates "noisy neighbor" problems — one service's heavy load (large scans, pipelining bursts, slow queries) impacts all other services sharing the same instance.

✅ Fix: Use separate Redis instances per service (or per functional domain). Use RESP3 client-set-name and monitoring to identify which service is causing load. In containerized environments, allocate dedicated instances per microservice.

items.label: Ignoring TTL — Memory Leaks
items.text:
Storing data in Redis without setting TTLs is the #1 cause of out-of-memory errors. Every key that doesn't expire stays in memory forever — until maxmemory is reached and Redis starts evicting (or rejecting writes).

✅ Fix: Every cached item should have a TTL. Use SETEX or explicitly call EXPIRE. Monitor total keys with INFO keyspace and set up alerts for unexpected key count growth. Use maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru as a safety net, but don't rely on it as your primary cleanup mechanism.

items.label: Blocking Operations on the Main Thread
items.text:
Several operations block the Redis main thread, preventing ALL other clients from executing commands:
• KEYS * — O(N) scan
• SMEMBERS on large sets — returns ALL members
• FLUSHALL / FLUSHDB (sync) — deletes everything
• SORT on large lists/sets
• ZRANGEBYSCORE without LIMIT
• BRPOPLPUSH with large data
• MONITOR — reduces throughput by ~50%

✅ Fix: Use SCAN family (SCAN, SSCAN, HSCAN, ZSCAN) for iteration. Use UNLINK instead of DEL for large keys (async deletion). Use FLUSHALL ASYNC. Always add LIMIT to range queries.

rows: Redis = cache, DB = source of truth
No auth, no limits, no persistence

sections.title: Summary & Best Practices
sections.description: Key takeaways, production checklist, and next steps for mastering Redis/Valkey.

items.label: Key Takeaways
text: Redis is an in-memory data structure store — not just a cache. It supports strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes, streams, bitmaps, HyperLogLog, and geospatial indices.

Redis 8.0 introduces the INCREX command (atomic counter + expiry), ARRAY data structure (efficient indexed lists), and Hash Field Expiration (per-field TTLs). Redis also returned to AGPLv3 open-source licensing in 2025.
Valkey is the open-source fork of Redis under BSD 3-Clause license, governed by the Linux Foundation. It's a drop-in replacement with added search capabilities in version 9.x.
Choose your data type wisely — Hashes for objects, Sorted Sets for leaderboards, Streams for message queues, Bitmaps for boolean tracking, HyperLogLog for unique counting.
Persistence is configurable — Use hybrid (RDB + AOF) for production, RDB-only for cache, AOF-everysec for critical data, or disable entirely for pure cache.
Replication + Sentinel provides high availability — 1 master, 2+ replicas, 3+ Sentinels for automatic failover.
Redis Cluster shards data across nodes using 16,384 hash slots — ideal for datasets larger than single-node RAM.
ACLs replace the old single-password model — create users with specific command and key permissions.
Avoid KEYS *, FLUSHALL, and other dangerous commands in production — rename or disable them in redis.conf.
Monitor performance with SLOWLOG, INFO commandstats, latency-monitor-threshold, and Prometheus/Grafana.
Design for failure — test failover scenarios, practice disaster recovery, and always have a backup strategy.

items.label: Production Readiness Checklist
text: ☐ Set requirepass or configure ACLs — Never run Redis without authentication
☐ Bind to specific interfaces — Never 0.0.0.0 in production
☐ Configure protected-mode yes
☐ Rename or disable dangerous commands (FLUSHALL, FLUSHDB, CONFIG, DEBUG, SHUTDOWN)
☐ Set maxmemory with appropriate eviction policy
☐ Enable persistence (hybrid RDB + AOF recommended)
☐ Set up replication with at least 2 replicas
☐ Configure Sentinel (3+ instances) for automatic failover
☐ Set up Prometheus redis_exporter + Grafana monitoring
☐ Configure slowlog-log-slower-than and monitor regularly
☐ Set up automated backups (RDB snapshots) with retention policy
☐ Test backup restoration procedure
☐ Disable Transparent Huge Pages (THP) on the host OS
☐ Set vm.overcommit_memory=1 in sysctl
☐ Increase max open files (ulimit -n 65536) for high-traffic deployments
☐ Use connection pooling in application clients
☐ Implement circuit breakers for Redis connection failures
☐ Document key naming conventions (app:module:entity:id:field)
☐ Set up alerts for: replication lag, memory pressure, OOM errors, failover events
☐ Review SLOWLOG weekly — identify and optimize slow queries
☐ Conduct failover drills quarterly
☐ Keep Redis/Valkey version up to date (subscribe to security advisories)

items.label: Next Steps
text: 1. Install Redis or Valkey locally and work through every command in this guide — hands-on practice is the best teacher.
2. Build a caching layer — Add Redis caching to an existing application. Monitor the cache hit ratio improvement.
3. Implement a real-time leaderboard — Use Sorted Sets for a game or application that needs ranked data.
4. Set up Redis Sentinel — Configure 3 Sentinels and practice killing the master to test automatic failover.
5. Deploy Redis Cluster — Start with 6 nodes (3 masters + 3 replicas) and practice resharding.
6. Migrate to Valkey — Test Valkey 9.x as a drop-in replacement and explore its built-in search features.
7. Monitor and optimize — Set up Prometheus/Grafana monitoring. Tune the slow log, eviction policy, and memory settings based on real metrics.
8. Explore Redis Stack — Add RedisJSON for document storage and RediSearch for full-text search in your application.
9. Implement a message queue — Use Redis Streams with consumer groups to build a reliable task processing pipeline.
10. Go deeper — Read the Redis documentation at redis.io/docs, explore the Valkey project at valkey.io, and study the source code to understand the internals.
